# The three-organization model {#sec-three-orgs}

The workshop's material is spread across three GitHub organizations, not one. This looks like more than you need at first, but each organization has a distinct job, and keeping them separate is what makes the course reusable and citable over time.

Throughout this chapter, `gitforsci` stands in for your course's short name. Replace it with your own slug everywhere (for example a course named `gitforsci` would have organizations `gitforsci`, `gitforsci-dev`, and `gitforsci-001`).

TODO: see part-setup.qmd and my comment about the github org that a new instructor needs. I have also added a previous comment that this guide should not use "course" placeholder but use gitforsci directly.

## The three organizations

TODO: Rework based on the new information. Focus on what a new instructor needs to do rather than explaning the whole concept in too much detail.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    subgraph central[course -- central org]
        CW[website<br/>handbook, canonical citation, DOI]
    end
    subgraph dev[course-dev -- development org]
        DT[website template]
        DM[setup, admin, exercises, assignments]
    end
    subgraph iter[course-00x -- iteration org]
        IW[website<br/>generated from template]
        IM[per-cohort repos]
    end
    dev -->|generate fresh each cohort| iter
    central -.->|canonical citation copied down| dev
    central -.->|canonical citation copied down| iter
```

### The central org: `gitforsci`

The long-lived, public face of the course. There is one central org, ever, not one per cohort. It holds:

- The `website` repository, which is **this handbook** plus the course landing page. TODO: It does not hold the course landing page, the course website is in gitforsci-dev/website from which the template is used.

- The **canonical `CITATION.cff`** and the course-level DOI. This is the single source of truth for how the course is cited (@sec-citation-license). TODO: Think critically whether there should a DOI for each iteration. One DOI for all material gitforsci/* and gitforsci-dev/*, but then a second DOI for each iteration (e.g. gitforsci-cis, gitforsci-ghe). Provide me with pros and cons. 

TODO: The central org website provides all information to run the course, however, it also evolves over time and each iteration provides an opportunity for learning. This website should actually have a section (towards the end of the book or appendix) which documents each iteration. Start with: https://github.com/gitforsci-cis


### The development org: `gitforsci-dev`

TODO: A new instructor should be added to the org as a contributor and through PRs be able to improve material. This needs a CONTRIBUTING.md guide for this specific GitHub org. The idea is not that the material is changed siginificantly between iterations, but should allow for opportunities to improve over time. 

Where you author and maintain the teaching material between cohorts. It holds the **templates** that each new cohort is generated from:

- A `website` **template repository**: the starting point for each cohort's site.
- Material repositories: the setup instructions, any admin material, and the exercise and assignment templates.

TODO: Revise next paragraph based on TODO above.
When you improve the material, you do it here. Those improvements reach future cohorts because each new cohort is generated fresh from these templates.

### The iteration org: `gitforsci-00x`

One organization per time you teach the course. Name it with an iteration tag: `gitforsci-001`, `gitforsci-002`, or something descriptive like `gitforsci-2026spring`. It holds:

- A `website` **generated from the `gitforsci-dev` template**. This is an independent copy with its own clean history and no live link back to the template. TODO: Here would be the consideration to give it it's own DOI. I have setup a task for you to research and assess.

- Per-cohort copies of the material repositories your learners will work in.

Each iteration org is a frozen snapshot: the course exactly as you taught it that time. Learners are members of the iteration org, but not of the organizations.

## How material flows

Two flows matter, and they go in different directions.

**Templates flow forward.** When you generate a new cohort from `gitforsci-dev`, it picks up every improvement you have made to the templates since the last cohort. Past cohorts do not change; they are frozen. Only future cohorts inherit your edits. If you ever need to carry a fix into a cohort that already exists, you apply it there as an ordinary commit.

**Citation flows down.** The canonical `CITATION.cff` lives in the central `gitforsci/website`. When it changes (a new author, a corrected ORCID, a new DOI), you copy it down into the other repositories so everything cites the course the same way. @sec-citation-license covers this. TODO: re-asses with the new information you have.

## Why not just one organization

Splitting the central org from the development org keeps your public identity (the citation, the DOI, the handbook) stable while the messy work of editing material happens elsewhere.

Splitting the development org from each iteration org lets template improvements flow forward to future cohorts without back-contaminating the template with one cohort's specific dates, names, and URLs. Each past cohort stays exactly as it was taught, which is what makes it a trustworthy archival record.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**New educator** — you do not need all three on day one. To teach the course once, you need an iteration org with a website and the material repositories. The central and development orgs pay off when you teach it a second time and want a clean way to reuse and cite the material. @sec-create-orgs builds them in an order that makes this practical.
:::
